content.problemStatement:
A binary string SSS of length NNN is called KKK-balanced string if it holds the following property: For each index i  (1≤i≤N)i\;(1 \leq i \leq N)i(1≤i≤N) such that Si=S_i = Si​= '1', there must exist an index j  (1≤j≤N)j\;(1 \leq j \leq N)j(1≤j≤N) such that Sj=S_j = Sj​= '1'  and ∣i−j∣=K\mid i - j \mid = K∣i−j∣=K, where ∣x∣\mid x \mid∣x∣ denotes the absolute value of xxx.

For example, if K=1K = 1K=1, "00", "110", "011011" are 1-balanced strings, while "010" , "1001" are not; if K=2K = 2K=2, "10101", "0101000101" are 2-balanced strings, while "0110" , "1001", "010" are not.

Chef is given a binary string SSS of length NNN and an integer KKK. He can perform the following operation on the string:


Select an index of the string, and flip the character at that index. This means if the character was "0", it becomes "1", and vice versa.
What is the minimum number of operations Chef will have to perform so that the string becomes KKK-balanced?


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N,KN, KN,K.
The second line of each test case contains a string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the minimum number operation Chef needs to perform on the given string to make it KKK-balanced.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef applies one operation on the index 222 and the string becomes "00" which is a 1-balanced string.
Test case 222: The given string is already 1-balanced.
Test case 333: Chef applies one operation on the index 333 and the string becomes "1111" which is a 2-balanced string.
Test case 444: Chef applies one operation on the index 222 and another operation on the index 333. Hence, the string becomes "10010" which is a 3-balanced string.
